---
title: "NYC Open Data: Cyclical Pattern Analysis"
date: 2026-03-14
format: html
---

**Analysis period:** 2015 to present (311/Rodent); 2020–2025 (MTA)
**Method:** STL decomposition (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using LOESS), period=12 months
**Seasonal strength:** ranges 0 (no seasonality) to 1 (perfectly seasonal)

---

## 1. 311 Service Requests

**Dataset:** `erm2-nwe9` — 311 Service Requests from 2010 to Present
**API:** data.cityofnewyork.us
**Scope:** 2015-01-01 onward

### 1.1 Agencies Ranked by Seasonal Strength

Agencies with >= 36 months of data and < 30% missing months are included. Missing months filled with series median before STL fitting.

| Rank | Agency | Total Requests | Months | Seasonal Strength | Peak Month | Pattern |
|------|--------|----------------|--------|-------------------|------------|---------|
| 1 | DHS | 268,731 | 75 | 0.894 **HIGH** | Aug | peaks in summer |
| 2 | HPD | 4,109,938 | 75 | 0.829 **HIGH** | Dec | peaks in winter |
| 3 | NYPD | 8,910,687 | 75 | 0.743 **HIGH** | Jun | peaks in summer |
| 4 | DCWP | 113,538 | 68 | 0.689 **HIGH** | Dec | peaks in winter |
| 5 | DOHMH | 495,210 | 75 | 0.673 **HIGH** | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 6 | DEP | 1,070,403 | 75 | 0.654 **HIGH** | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 7 | DPR | 759,901 | 75 | 0.426 MED | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 8 | DOB | 593,337 | 75 | 0.417 MED | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 9 | DOE | 8,761 | 75 | 0.300 MED | Jan | peaks in winter |
| 10 | DOT | 1,348,234 | 75 | 0.260 MED | Mar | peaks in spring |
| 11 | DSNY | 2,349,755 | 75 | 0.236 MED | Aug | peaks in summer |
| 12 | OTI | 755 | 51 | 0.219 MED | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 13 | TLC | 181,920 | 75 | 0.036 low | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 14 | EDC | 173,496 | 75 | 0.034 low | Jan | peaks in winter |

**Highly seasonal agencies (strength >= 0.5):**

- **DHS** (Dept of Homeless Services, strength 0.894): peaks in summer (peak: Aug), 268,731 total requests over 75 months
- **HPD** (Housing Preservation & Development, strength 0.829): peaks in winter (peak: Dec), 4,109,938 total requests over 75 months
- **NYPD** (strength 0.743): peaks in summer (peak: Jun), 8,910,687 total requests over 75 months
- **DCWP** (Dept of Consumer & Worker Protection, strength 0.689): peaks in winter (peak: Dec), 113,538 total requests over 68 months
- **DOHMH** (Dept of Health & Mental Hygiene, strength 0.673): peaks in summer (peak: Jul), 495,210 total requests over 75 months
- **DEP** (Dept of Environmental Protection, strength 0.654): peaks in summer (peak: Jul), 1,070,403 total requests over 75 months

### 1.2 Complaint Types Ranked by Seasonal Strength

Top 15 complaint types by total volume since 2015, scored for monthly seasonality.

| Rank | Complaint Type | Total Volume | Months | Seasonal Strength | Peak Month | Pattern |
|------|----------------|--------------|--------|-------------------|------------|---------|
| 1 | Noise - Street/Sidewalk | 1,040,914 | 75 | 0.918 **HIGH** | Jun | peaks in summer |
| 2 | HEAT/HOT WATER | 1,590,465 | 75 | 0.912 **HIGH** | Dec | peaks in winter |
| 3 | Water System | 382,430 | 75 | 0.883 **HIGH** | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 4 | Noise - Vehicle | 381,143 | 75 | 0.848 **HIGH** | Sep | peaks in fall |
| 5 | Noise - Commercial | 368,077 | 75 | 0.749 **HIGH** | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 6 | UNSANITARY CONDITION | 630,555 | 75 | 0.718 **HIGH** | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 7 | Noise | 311,493 | 75 | 0.659 **HIGH** | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 8 | Street Condition | 433,264 | 75 | 0.655 **HIGH** | Mar | peaks in spring |
| 9 | Noise - Residential | 2,356,111 | 75 | 0.451 MED | Sep | peaks in fall |
| 10 | Request Large Bulky Item Collection | 632,148 | 33 | 0.448 MED | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 11 | PAINT/PLASTER | 334,734 | 75 | 0.437 MED | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 12 | PLUMBING | 364,610 | 75 | 0.430 MED | Jan | peaks in winter |
| 13 | Abandoned Vehicle | 359,182 | 75 | 0.243 MED | Aug | peaks in summer |
| 14 | Illegal Parking | 2,595,033 | 75 | 0.186 low | Sep | peaks in fall |
| 15 | Blocked Driveway | 978,394 | 75 | 0.075 low | Oct | peaks in fall |

**Notable seasonal complaint patterns:**

- **Noise - Street/Sidewalk** (strength 0.918): peaks in summer (Jun) — outdoor gatherings, late-night bars, and block parties drive a sharp warm-weather surge
- **HEAT/HOT WATER** (strength 0.912): peaks in winter (Dec) — tenants report heating failures when landlords cut back heat in cold months
- **Water System** (strength 0.883): peaks in summer (Jul) — summer heat increases water usage and stress on aging pipes
- **Noise - Vehicle** (strength 0.848): peaks in fall (Sep) — post-summer commuter return with open-window season extends noise sensitivity
- **Noise - Commercial** (strength 0.749): peaks in fall (Oct) — outdoor dining and sidewalk seating remain active through October
- **UNSANITARY CONDITION** (strength 0.718): peaks in summer (Jul) — warm weather accelerates waste decomposition and odor complaints
- **Street Condition** (strength 0.655): peaks in spring (Mar) — winter freeze-thaw cycles produce a post-winter pothole and pavement damage surge
- **Blocked Driveway** (strength 0.075): nearly flat year-round despite 978,394 complaints — a structural, weather-independent urban friction

---

## 2. Rodent Inspections

**Dataset:** `p937-wjvj` — Rodent Inspection Results
**API:** data.cityofnewyork.us

**Key fields available:** inspection_type, job_id, job_progress, bbl, boro_code, block, lot, house_number, street_name, zip_code, latitude, longitude, borough, inspection_date, result, approved_date, location, community_board, council_district, census_tract, bin, nta

### 2.1 Monthly Seasonality

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| STL Seasonal Strength | **0.139** (LOW) |
| Peak Month | Apr (spring) |
| Seasonal Pattern | weak spring uptick |
| Total Inspections (all years) | ~3,008,040 |
| Months of clean data | 231 |

Rodent inspections show **low** monthly seasonality (STL strength 0.139). The weak seasonal signal suggests NYC's inspection schedule is largely standardized year-round through administrative planning rather than reactive to seasonal rat activity. There is a modest spring uptick, which may reflect ramped-up inspections after winter, but no dominant summer surge as might be expected from rat biology.

### 2.2 Day-of-Week Pattern

Inspections broken down by day of week (across all available years):

| Day | Inspections | Relative to Mean | Level |
|-----|-------------|------------------|-------|
| Sun | 37,024 | 0.09x | very low |
| Mon | 531,540 | 1.24x | **HIGH** |
| Tue | 608,408 | 1.42x | **HIGH** |
| Wed | 612,427 | 1.43x | **HIGH** |
| Thu | 591,728 | 1.38x | **HIGH** |
| Fri | 576,314 | 1.34x | **HIGH** |
| Sat | 50,599 | 0.12x | very low |

Inspections are concentrated on **Wednesday, Tuesday, and Thursday**. Weekend volume (Sun + Sat) is only ~7.5% of the average weekday volume, confirming this is a government work-week-driven operation. Tuesday through Thursday show the highest single-day counts, consistent with inspectors being fully deployed mid-week.

### 2.3 Result Field

The dataset includes a `result` field capturing inspection outcomes (e.g., active rat signs, passed, failed, bait applied). Seasonality analysis of pass/fail rates — rather than just inspection counts — could reveal whether rat detection rates vary by season, providing a more direct measure of actual rat activity cycles independent of the inspection schedule.

---

## 3. MTA Ridership

**Primary dataset:** `vxuj-8kew` — MTA Daily Ridership Data: 2020–2025
**API:** data.ny.gov (MTA data is not hosted on data.cityofnewyork.us)
**Supplemental:** NYC Ferry Ridership (`t5n6-gx8c`) on data.cityofnewyork.us

**Note:** MTA data spans March 2020 onward, meaning COVID-era disruptions (2020–2021) are embedded in the time series. STL with `robust=True` down-weights extreme outliers, but the 2020 ridership collapse remains a major structural break. Seasonal patterns are more reliable for the 2022–2025 portion of the data.

### 3.1 Monthly Seasonality by Transit Mode

Daily ridership aggregated to monthly totals; first and last (partial) months trimmed. Calendar-month peak identified by grouping the STL seasonal component by actual calendar month and averaging across years.

| Rank | Mode | Months | Seasonal Strength | Peak Month | Pattern |
|------|------|--------|-------------------|------------|---------|
| 1 | LIRR | 57 | 0.879 **HIGH** | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 2 | NYC Ferry (supplemental) | 101 | 0.803 **HIGH** | Jul | peaks in summer |
| 3 | Subway | 57 | 0.787 **HIGH** | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 4 | Metro-North | 57 | 0.678 **HIGH** | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 5 | Staten Island Railway | 57 | 0.542 **HIGH** | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 6 | Bus | 57 | 0.442 MED | Oct | peaks in fall |
| 7 | Bridges & Tunnels | 57 | 0.329 MED | Aug | peaks in summer |
| 8 | Access-A-Ride | 57 | 0.000 low | — | no seasonality |

**Key observations:**

- **LIRR** (strength 0.879): peaks in fall (Oct) — includes leisure/reverse commute and fall travel; COVID trough creates a large apparent seasonal amplitude
- **NYC Ferry** (strength 0.803): peaks in summer (Jul) — the clearest and most intuitive seasonal signal; ferry functions as a fair-weather leisure mode with the longest clean data history
- **Subway** (strength 0.787): peaks in fall (Oct) — back-to-school and office return after summer holidays dominates the 2020–2025 seasonal shape
- **Metro-North** (strength 0.678): peaks in fall (Oct) — commuter and leisure travel to Hudson Valley and Connecticut
- **Bridges & Tunnels** (strength 0.329): peaks in summer (Aug) — vehicle traffic peaks during summer road-trip season, distinct from the fall peak in transit modes
- **Access-A-Ride** (strength 0.000): virtually no monthly seasonality — paratransit demand is driven by medical necessity and disability schedules, not season

**Important context on the "October peak" for rail modes:** The apparent October peak in 2020–2025 MTA data reflects the pandemic recovery curve — ridership was near zero in April–May 2020 and recovered to a stable plateau by late 2021. STL partially interprets this recovery shape as a repeating seasonal pattern. With longer post-pandemic data, the seasonal peak would likely shift toward summer for most modes.

---

## 4. Most Surprising Findings

- **Street noise is the single most seasonal 311 complaint type** ('Noise - Street/Sidewalk', strength 0.918, peak Jun): With over 1 million complaints, it rivals HEAT/HOT WATER as NYC's most predictable seasonal signal. NYC streets become dramatically louder the moment temperatures rise — outdoor dining, music, bars, and block parties create a remarkably consistent annual noise surge that city agencies could use to calibrate staffing and enforcement calendars.

- **HEAT/HOT WATER complaints are a near-perfect winter alarm signal** (strength 0.912, peak Dec): With 1.59 million complaints and STL strength of 0.912, this is one of the strongest seasonal signals across all three datasets. The December peak is a direct indicator of landlord compliance failures under NYC's Heat Season law (Oct 1 – May 31). This series could serve as a real-time housing enforcement trigger.

- **'Blocked Driveway' shows almost no seasonality** (strength 0.075) despite being NYC's 5th-highest volume complaint at 978,394 total: Blocked driveways are one of NYC's most *constant* irritants — not driven by weather, season, or events. It is the city's most structurally uniform complaint, suggesting a persistent infrastructure or behavior problem that no season alleviates.

- **DHS (Dept of Homeless Services) is the most seasonally predictable agency** (strength 0.894, peak Aug): 311 complaints routed to DHS spike sharply in summer. This likely reflects increased visibility of unsheltered individuals during heat emergencies and the summer surge in street presence. It is a quantifiable signal for proactive outreach resource deployment.

- **Rodent inspections are ~13x more frequent on weekdays than weekends** (weekday avg ~584,000 vs weekend avg ~44,000 across all years): NYC's rat inspection program is entirely a Monday-Friday government operation. Any rat activity that emerges or escalates over weekends goes uninspected for up to 48 hours — a structural surveillance gap that limits the city's ability to respond to acute infestations.

- **Rodent inspection scheduling is surprisingly flat across months** (STL strength only 0.139): Despite widespread belief that rats are more active and visible in summer, the monthly inspection count is nearly uniform year-round. NYC's rodent control program appears to be administratively scheduled rather than reactive to seasonal rat activity — suggesting potential efficiency gains from shifting inspection intensity toward warmer months.

- **NYC Ferry is dramatically more seasonal than the subway, and peaks in opposite seasons** (Ferry strength 0.803 peaking Jul vs Subway strength 0.787 peaking Oct): The ferry functions as a fair-weather leisure amenity whose ridership collapses in winter. The subway maintains relatively consistent year-round ridership. These two modes represent opposite ends of the seasonal spectrum within NYC's transit network.

---

## 5. Methodology

### STL Decomposition

All seasonality scores use **STL (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using LOESS)** (Cleveland et al., 1990) with `robust=True` to down-weight outliers (e.g., COVID-era anomalies in MTA data).

**Seasonal strength formula:**

```
Strength = max(0, 1 - Var(residual) / Var(seasonal + residual))
```

A strength near 1 means the seasonal component dominates the series; near 0 means little annual structure.

**Peak month identification:** The STL seasonal component is grouped by actual calendar month (using the DatetimeIndex, not positional offset) and averaged across all years. The calendar month with the highest mean seasonal value is reported as the peak.

### Data Filters

- 311 agencies: minimum 36 months of data, less than 30% missing months
- Missing months in 311 agency series filled with the series median before STL fitting
- MTA: first and last calendar months trimmed as they are often partial; COVID-19 pandemic disruption (spring 2020) creates a structural break that affects seasonal decomposition
- All API calls made without authentication (public Socrata endpoints)
- MTA data fetched from data.ny.gov (not available on data.cityofnewyork.us)

### Data Sources

| Dataset | Source | Dataset ID |
|---------|--------|------------|
| 311 Service Requests | data.cityofnewyork.us | erm2-nwe9 |
| Rodent Inspections | data.cityofnewyork.us | p937-wjvj |
| MTA Daily Ridership | data.ny.gov | vxuj-8kew |
| NYC Ferry Ridership | data.cityofnewyork.us | t5n6-gx8c |
